critic_raw:
This RL route optimization experiment has four issues that compromise the validity of any positive finding.

1. Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset is explicit data leakage. The design states: 'all preprocessing transformers...are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' This applies to traffic encoders and weather encoders. Test-set traffic and weather statistics are incorporated into the normalization and encoding parameters seen by both models during training and inference. The stated justification — 'mirror production environments where model drift is avoided by consistent feature scaling' — is wrong. In production, a model is trained on historical data and applied to future data; the future data's statistics are not available at training time. Evidence: refit all transformers on training data only; if metric changes materially on the test set, the design's conclusions are unreliable.

2. The RL agent is trained in simulation, not on the actual delivery problem. The DQN is trained on '100k simulated episodes parameterized by training-set traffic patterns.' This means the agent learns a policy for a simulation model of the routing environment, then is evaluated on real deliveries. The simulation-to-real gap — whether the simulated reward landscape matches actual delivery dynamics, whether the discrete waypoint action space covers realistic routing decisions — is not assessed. A DQN that achieves low validation loss in simulation may systematically fail on real data due to simulator misspecification. Evidence: report the correlation between simulated episode returns and actual test-set delivery times for the same routes; low correlation indicates the simulation does not faithfully represent the real task.

3. Comparison fairness is broken by tuning asymmetry. The RL agent undergoes 'hyperparameter search over 20 random configurations'; the logistic regression baseline uses 'default regularization strength (C=1.0)' with explicit justification that this 'avoids the risk of overfitting the baseline to a specific validation window.' This is not tuning parity — it is deliberate undertuning of the baseline. A well-tuned logistic regression on route features typically outperforms an untuned version by a substantial margin, particularly with interaction features like distance × congestion included. Evidence: tune the logistic regression baseline with equivalent search effort (20 random configurations over C and feature subsets); report the performance gap before and after tuning the baseline. If the gap narrows, the RL advantage was partially attributable to baseline undertuning.

4. Single-city, single-year evaluation prevents any generalization claim. The test period is October-December of year Y from a single metropolitan area. Holiday-season congestion patterns are acknowledged as potentially anomalous but not corrected for. If the RL agent was trained on January-June data (pre-holiday) and the test period is holiday-heavy, the agent may fail systematically on conditions it never saw during training. The 'secondary analysis stratified by holiday proximity' is mentioned but not specified as a pass/fail criterion. Evidence: define in advance whether holiday-period performance below a threshold constitutes failure of the hypothesis; if not, the secondary analysis is post-hoc and cannot rescue a failed primary finding.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting — direct data leakage of test-set traffic and weather statistics into training
RL agent trained in simulation, not on real routing problem — simulation-to-real gap is unassessed
Tuning asymmetry: RL agent receives 20-configuration hyperparameter search; logistic regression baseline uses default C=1.0 (deliberately undertuned)
Single-city, single-year with holiday-heavy test period — potential distribution shift not addressed as a pass/fail criterion
Single-city evaluation prevents any generalization claim; city-specific factors dominate routing performance
Offline-trained RL agent cannot exploit real-time adaptation, which is RL's primary advantage over heuristics
Temporal split within a single calendar year confounds evaluation with seasonal drift patterns
Reward function and evaluation metric alignment gives RL a structural advantage unrelated to policy quality
Heuristic baseline is unspecified and likely not state-of-the-art; comparison may be against a straw-man
Baseline is intentionally not hyperparameter-tuned while RL agent is; comparison does not control for tuning effort
RL agent trained on simulator of unvalidated fidelity; policy may be optimized for simulator artifacts rather than real traffic dynamics
Test period (Oct-Dec) includes holiday distribution shift not present in training (Jan-Jun); secondary analysis documents but does not resolve confound
t-test on route-level delivery times ignores spatial and temporal autocorrelation, inflating false positive rate
